column1: What happens when the user changes their goal mid-program. The engine re-runs the parts of the Generator Flow that depend on goal (split selection, volume budget, prescription) while preserving everything that does not (logged loads, progression history, schedule, screening). The user's strength gains and history are never thrown away by a goal change, and intensity never spikes: a single easy transition week absorbs the shift. A goal change always creates a new program version.

column3: User tells the coach they want a different goal, or edits it in settings
column4: Confirm the new canonical goal (Strength, Hypertrophy, Fat Loss, General Fitness) and that they want to change now versus at the next block.
column5: Ask once, do not nag. The user owns their goal.

column3: New goal may change the best split (e.g. Fat Loss favours a conditioning day, Hypertrophy favours PPL or focus)
column4: Re-run the Split Selector with the new goal and the user's existing days and experience. If the split_id changes, show the user the new structure before applying.
column5: The calendar and commitments carry over, only the day structure may change.

column3: Weekly set targets differ by goal
column4: Re-read Weekly Volume for the new goal and experience. Re-run reconciliation. Re-apply focal points and commitment cuts.
column5: Volume shifts gradually, do not spike total sets more than 20 percent week over week.

column3: Each exercise's sets, reps, RIR and rest come from the Goal|Class grid row
column4: Look up the new Goal|Class prescription for every exercise in the program. Re-clamp against Min RIR, Failure Allowed and all Safety Rules.
column5: Exercise selection can largely stay; the prescription around it changes.

column3: A user moving to Fat Loss should not lose the muscle they built
column4: Hold load where the grid allows, cut volume before load (Fat Loss rule), and keep the main compounds in the program even as accessory volume drops.
column5: Load is the last thing cut, always.

column3: The user's logged loads and history stay valid
column4: Do not reset progression_state on a goal change. Current loads carry into the new prescription; only the rep and set targets around them move.
column5: History is never wiped by a goal change.

column3: Big jumps in style (e.g. General Fitness to Strength) change intensity a lot
column4: Ease into the new intensity over one week: apply the new structure but keep rir_min one higher than target for the first week, then settle.
column5: One easy transition week protects against a sudden intensity spike.

column3: A goal change is a natural moment to confirm nothing health-relevant has changed
column4: Ask if anything about their health has changed since onboarding. If yes, re-run the screening flow before applying the new goal.
column5: Screening stays current across the life of the program.

column4: A goal change creates a new program version (Data Schemas: programs.version up, superseded_by set on the old one). The old program stays in history.
column5: Nothing is destructive. The user can see what changed and when.
